ProjectDawn Security Review (2026-02-09). CRITICAL: (1) split() propagates PERMANENTLY_LOCKED flag — no extraction bypass but confusing. (2) move_stake() does NOT check PERMANENTLY_LOCKED — destination gets StakeFlags::empty() (stake_state.rs:859,876) — REAL BYPASS, locked stake moves to unlocked account. (3) move_lamports() unchecked but only moves free lamports. HIGH: (4) deactivate_delinquent() ignores lock — anyone can force-deactivate. (5) fee split interpolation may overflow (safe in practice). (6) split_fees() overflows if total_fees>1.8e15 lamports. MEDIUM: (7) set_lockup() allows lockup changes on locked accounts. (8) authorize() allows authority transfer on locked accounts. (9) bank.rs:3672 += unchecked. (10) saturating_mul in penalty calc. LOW: passive_tier bits 6-7 OOB on array index, merge propagates lock, treasury no audit trail. GovernanceUnlock stubbed (safe for launch).

AdenHQ/Hive Judge System (Feb 23 2026). Layer 1: EventLoopNode implicit judge — post-execution per LLM turn, verdicts ACCEPT/RETRY/ESCALATE. Default rule-based: ACCEPT if no tool calls + output_keys present. LLMJudge: LiteLLM provider-agnostic. Level 2 (v0.4.4): evaluates full conversation vs success_criteria. judge_every_n_turns=1, max_iterations=50, max_retries=3. RETRY injects feedback as system msg. Layer 2: HybridJudge (ODD/goal-level) — verdicts ACCEPT/RETRY/REPLAN/ESCALATE. REPLAN at <90% weighted score. SuccessCriterion: id,description,metric(output_contains/equals/llm_judge/custom),target,weight. Constraints: hard→ESCALATE, soft→REPLAN+warn; categories: time/cost/safety/scope/quality. Evolution cycle: execute→evaluate→diagnose→regenerate (coding agent rewrites graph/prompts/edges). Key files: event_loop_node.py, node.py (NodeSpec), executor.py (GraphExecutor).

Gate Architecture Comparison (Session 288). Current Option C: 17 Python gates, 5,900 lines, 102ms/call breakdown: 50ms subprocess fork + 22ms Python startup + 24ms imports + 3ms state I/O + 0.4ms all 17 gates. Gate logic 0.4ms — bottleneck is Python startup. Scored 88/130. Option A (7 bash+4 Python+5 CLAUDE.md rules): 980 lines, bash 6ms vs Python 108ms, scored 79/130. Migration breaks 50+ files (27 shared modules, 15 skills, analytics_server 126 refs). Option B (9 bash+7 CLAUDE.md): 6ms overhead, 19s/session saved, scored 63/130. Loses: memory enforcement G4/G6/G9/G15, injection defense G17 (771-line homoglyph/base64/nested-JSON), audit trail, 1,022 gate tests. KEY: reducing 17 to 6 Python gates saves only 0.3ms (irrelevant). Option C wins — 102ms is price of MCP enforcement, injection defense, audit trail, 288 sessions proven.

GSD vs Torus context management. GSD (Fresh-Per-Task): 200K fresh context per task (zero noise), thin orchestrator 30-40%, PROJECT.md/STATE.md/REQUIREMENTS.md/ROADMAP.md explicitly passed, .planning/ dir file-based handoff, plans 2-3 tasks each ~50% subagent context, Task 1=Task 50 quality. Torus (Accumulated-Session): 185+ sessions, SubagentStart injects LIVE_STATE.json+state_*.json (files read, errors, test status, banned strategies, per-agent-type hints), Memory MCP cross-session, 17 gates for context rot prevention. Context rot: GSD prevents via fresh start, Torus manages via selective state injection. State carryover: GSD explicit .planning/ files; Torus semantic memory + LIVE_STATE + SubagentStart. Pattern to adopt from GSD: reduce subagent injection to 50% target, explicit planning dir handoff, wave-based parallelism.

SVM vs EVM DEX architecture. ACCOUNT MODEL: EVM code+storage per contract, factory per pool; SVM stateless programs, state in accounts, pools as PDA-derived Token Accounts, no factory. TX CONSTRAINTS: Solana 1,232 bytes, ~35 accounts/tx, 1.4M CUs/tx, 48M/block; DEX swaps 100k-300k CUs. STATE: EVM SLOAD/SSTORE; SVM accounts declared upfront for Sealevel parallel exec — why tick/bin arrays pre-loaded. CPI: SVM explicit cross-program invocations, PDAs sign without private key (vs EVM CALL/DELEGATECALL). EXECUTION: SVM parallel Sealevel; DAMM v2 fixed hot-account serialization bottleneck. LANGUAGE: SVM Rust/C to sBPF JIT (Anchor); EVM Solidity/Vyper to bytecode. DEX IMPLICATIONS: CLMM tick arrays 60 ticks/array+bitmap; DLMM bin arrays 70-100; swap routing must pre-calculate arrays; Raydium positions Token-2022 NFTs, Meteora DAMM v2 position NFTs; Token-2022 fee tokens need get_transfer_fee/get_transfer_inverse_fee.

Passive Staking: TRv1 vs eclipse-agave. TRv1: STANDALONE program (Pass1veStake1111...). Instructions: InitializePassiveStake, ClaimRewards, Unlock, EarlyUnlock, CalculateEpochRewards (crank per account per epoch). State: PassiveStakeAccount 84 bytes (authority,amount,lock_days,lock_start,lock_end,unclaimed_rewards,last_reward_epoch,is_permanent,vote_weight_bps). Tiers in days: 0/30/90/180/360/permanent. Reward rates BPS: 500/1000/2000/3000/5000/12000. Early unlock: 5x tier rate (permanent cannot). ECLIPSE-AGAVE: INTEGRATED into stake program — PassiveLock(tier) and PermanentLock as StakeInstruction variants. Uses standard StakeStateV2. Passive = Stake with voter_pubkey=Pubkey::default() sentinel. Tiers 1-5 only. Lockup in StakeFlags bits 4-6 + lockup.epoch (14/36/72/144 epochs). Tier reward BPS: 25/50/100/150/250. PermanentLock: 12000 BPS (6% APY), PERMANENTLY_LOCKED flag. Rewards automatic via runtime epoch calculation. Early unlock: 5*rate_bp*stake/10000 to treasury. GovernanceUnlock stubbed. KEY DIFFERENCES: standalone vs integrated, manual crank vs automatic, tier 0 allowed vs required 1-5, inline lamports vs epoch system.

Passive Staking Reward Mechanisms: TRv1 vs eclipse-agave. FORMULAS: TRv1: reward_per_epoch=(amount*validator_rate_bp*tier_rate_bps)/(10000*10000*365), assumes 365 epochs/year. Eclipse-agave: passive_epoch_reward=stake*rate_bp/(10000*146), assumes 146 epochs/year (2.5-day epochs). DIFFERENT EPOCH ASSUMPTIONS = DIFFERENT AMOUNTS. TIER RATES (BPS of validator rate): TRv1 tier0=500/tier1=1000/tier2=2000/tier3=3000/tier4=5000/permanent=12000. Eclipse tier1=25/tier2=50/tier3=100/tier4=150/tier5=250 (of 5% base APY). Actual eclipse APY (5% validator): tier1~0.09%/tier2~0.17%/tier3~0.34%/tier4~0.51%/tier5~0.86%/permanent~6%. EARLY UNLOCK PENALTY: both use 5x tier_rate * stake/10000 sent to treasury; permanent cannot early-unlock. EPOCH TRACKING: TRv1 last_reward_epoch per account (crank invokes CalculateEpochRewards); eclipse via runtime StakeHistory + passive_epoch_reward in calculate_stake_vote_rewards per epoch. REWARD STORAGE: TRv1 inline lamports in PassiveStakeAccount; eclipse standard epoch rewards distribution system.

eclipse-agave CPI patterns for Phase 5 (SDK 2.2.0). BUILTIN ENTRYPOINT: declare_process_instruction!(Entrypoint, DEFAULT_COMPUTE_UNITS, |invoke_context|{...}) — same across stake, system, treasury, governance. NATIVE INVOKE (builtin-to-builtin CPI): invoke_context.native_invoke(instruction.into(), &[signers]) -> Result<(),InstructionError>; internally prepare_instruction()+process_instruction(). TX CONTEXT ACCESS: invoke_context.transaction_context.get_current_instruction_context()?, check_number_of_instruction_accounts(n)?, try_borrow_instruction_account(tx_ctx,idx)? for read/write; get_key_of_account_at_index(idx)? for pubkey without borrow. ACCOUNT BORROWING LIFETIME: must drop (end scope {}) before reborrowing at different index. SIGNERS: pass Pubkeys explicitly in signers slice for builtin-to-builtin CPI; PDA signers derived in-code. BANK vs NATIVE: bank-level code (fee_distribution.rs) can call get_account() directly; native programs must use native_invoke(). OPTIONAL ACCOUNTS: if get_number_of_instruction_accounts()>=6 { Some(5) } else { None }. ACCOUNT OWNERSHIP CHECK: account.get_owner()==&expected_program_id before reading data.

eclipse-agave Stake Program Cross-Program Account Reading (2026-03-02). KEY: Stake program ONLY reads accounts explicitly passed in tx instruction account list — NO arbitrary Bank.get_account(). Access via InstructionContext.try_borrow_instruction_account(transaction_context, index). OPTIONAL ACCOUNTS PATTERN: if get_number_of_instruction_accounts()>=6 { Some(5) } else { None } — optional account without failing if absent. CROSS-PROGRAM PDA VERIFICATION (bank-level only, fee_distribution.rs:356): (1) derive PDA: Pubkey::find_program_address(&[b"emergency_unlock",stake_pubkey.as_ref()],&governance_id); (2) read account: self.get_account_with_fixed_root_no_cache(&marker_pda); (3) check discriminator data()[0]==0x55. This is BANK code not native program — bank can call get_account(). GOVERNANCE UNLOCK FLOW: bank reads governance PDA marker, verifies discriminator, then unlocks stake (no CPI needed from stake program side). PUBKEY WITHOUT BORROW: transaction_context.get_key_of_account_at_index(idx)? — pubkey only, no borrow.

Torus-web Vitest test patterns. CONFIG: node env, globals:true, __tests__/**/*.test.ts, @=project root, vitest 4.0.18+. MOCKING: vi.hoisted() BEFORE vi.mock() calls; MongoDB: vi.mock('@/lib/mongodb')+static method mocks (findOne/create/aggregate); Auth: vi.mock('@/lib/jwt-auth') mockResolvedValue({userId:'admin-1'}); global fetch: vi.stubGlobal. REQUESTS: NextRequest from 'next/server', new URL(path,'http://localhost:3000'), url.searchParams.set(), {method,headers,body:JSON.stringify()}. SETUP: vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach, mockResolvedValueOnce for sequential calls. No shared utils — each test file self-contained with inline createRequest() helper. Route handlers: import named GET/POST/PUT/DELETE from '@/app/api/...'.

Eclipse-agave emergency unlock governance marker. MARKER CREATION (programs/governance/src/processor.rs:568-589): PDA=[b"emergency_unlock",stake_pubkey], governance program owner, 41 bytes — discriminator=0x55, target_pubkey[1..33], epoch[33..41]. VALIDATION (stake_state.rs:382-412): owner==GOVERNANCE_PROGRAM_ID, len>=33, data[0]==0x55, data[1..33]==stake_pubkey → Result<bool>. DISPATCH: (1) Deactivate — optional marker at index 3, bypasses PERMANENTLY_LOCKED; (2) EarlyUnlock — optional marker at index 4; (3) GovernanceUnlock — 4 accounts [stake,marker,clock,staker], routes to deactivate (PERMANENTLY_LOCKED) or early_unlock (passive sentinel). BEHAVIOR: marker NOT consumed (stake program can't write governance-owned account), re-use prevented by state change Stake→Initialized; with marker: zero penalty, immediate unlock; without: tier-based penalty. BANK: is_emergency_unlocked() at fee_distribution.rs:367-374. FUTURE: governance transfers marker ownership to stake program for true one-time use.

Safari iPad Picture-in-Picture limitations (Mar 2026). (1) canvas.captureStream() NOT supported in Safari iOS/iPad (WebKit Bug #181663) — blocks canvas→video→PiP entirely. (2) Safari PiP API is webkit-specific: webkitSupportsPresentationMode + webkitSetPresentationMode('picture-in-picture'|'inline'); standard requestPictureInPicture() not available; supported iPad Safari 9+, iPhone iOS 14+; NOT in Web App standalone mode. (3) DocumentPictureInPicture API NOT supported in any Safari (Chrome/Edge v116+ only, cannot do arbitrary HTML overlay). (4) Media Session API: basic metadata support, limited PiP integration. (5) PiP restricted to standard HTML5 <video> elements, no arbitrary HTML, floating controls limited. BOTTOM LINE: canvas-based overlays with dynamic content NOT feasible in Safari — only static HTML5 video can be placed in PiP.

TRv1 vs eclipse-agave developer rewards (2026-03-02). TRV1 (full on-chain, programs/developer-rewards/): 8 instructions, ProgramRevenueConfig PDA + EpochFeeTracker PDA, anti-gaming (1000 CU min, 7d cooldown, 10% epoch cap), multi-recipient splits up to 10 via RevenueSplit{recipient,share_bps}, ClaimDeveloperFees permissionless, 36+ unit tests. Fees accumulate on-chain (auditable, claimable anytime). ECLIPSE-AGAVE (runtime-only, NO on-chain program): distribute_developer_fees() in fee_distribution.rs — reads program_fee_attribution HashMap<Pubkey,u64>, calculates proportional share, pays directly to upgrade authority pubkey in-slot; no claim mechanism, no on-chain record, no anti-gaming (no CU threshold, no cooldown, no epoch cap); fallback to treasury/burn if deposit fails. KEY DIFF: TRv1 fees auditable on-chain+claimable; eclipse-agave deposits in-slot with no claiming, splitting, or anti-gaming. ATTRIBUTION: Bank.program_fee_attribution RwLock<HashMap<Pubkey,u64>>.

Linux Speech-to-Text for Claude Code (research). TIER1 OFFLINE: nerd-dictation(VOSK)=single-file Python, ~1.5s cold start, --output=STDOUT, easy piping, lower accuracy; whisper.cpp=C++ <300ms startup, streaming 5-7x slower; faster-whisper(CTranslate2)=30-40% faster RTF, 1.8s startup, best accuracy/speed. TIER3 CLOUD: Deepgram=$0.0077/min streaming, <300ms latency; AssemblyAI=$0.0025/min (cheapest). RECOMMENDATION: nerd-dictation with faster-whisper backend; keyboard shortcuts+STDOUT->faster-whisper->pipe to Claude Code stdin; latency 2-3s/utterance; cost $0. Alt if latency critical: Deepgram API (~4/month at 1h/day).

Solana Architecture (Feb 2026). RUNTIME: Sealevel parallel execution; txs declare accounts upfront, non-conflicting run in parallel. Accounts: 5 fields (lamports, data, owner, executable, rent_epoch); max 10MB; only System Program creates. Programs: sBPF via LLVM; Anchor vs Pinocchio (perf). Anchor types: Signer, Account, Program, SystemAccount, UncheckedAccount, InterfaceAccount. Constraints: init, mut, seeds+bump, has_one, close, realloc. PDAs: deterministic off-curve keys (seeds+bump). TOKENS: Mint + Token account per owner; ATA = PDA(owner, mint). Token-2022: transfer fees, confidential transfers, interest-bearing. DEFI: AMM x*y=k; orderbooks rare on-chain. Fees: base 5000 lamports + compute units. Compute: 200k default, 1.4M max. Slot: ~400ms; epoch: ~2 days.

Claude Code notification + terminal title research (2026-03-10). BACKGROUND TASK NOTIFICATIONS: run_in_background=true → completion message injected into conversation: 'Background command completed (exit code 0)'. Mechanism: process exit monitoring + event injection into conversation context. NO separate escape sequence or OS signal. TERMINAL TITLE: Claude Code sets terminal title via OSC escape sequences during active work; resets to shell default on exit. NOTIFICATION BELLS: sends terminal bell on task completion if terminal supports it. IDLE STATE: after run_in_background completion, Claude reactivates from idle. No configurable notification hooks exposed to users. Behavior is internal to Claude Code runtime — not customizable via settings.json or hooks.

Pyright import analysis for test_framework.py (2026-02-24). ROOT CAUSE: Pyright cannot follow runtime sys.path.insert() calls. test_framework.py uses sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(__file__)) at L15 to put hooks/ on sys.path for bare imports; Pyright is static. TWO IMPORT CATEGORIES: 1. shared.* imports work — shared/ has __init__.py and hooks/ is root. 2. Direct gate imports (import gate_01_read_before_edit) fail Pyright — no package structure. SOLUTION OPTIONS: pyrightconfig.json extraPaths=['hooks']; per-file type:ignore; pyproject.toml pythonpath. CHOSEN: pyrightconfig.json extraPaths=['hooks']. Does not affect runtime behavior. Gate files remain importable. Zero false positives after fix.

Meteora DLMM. ID: LBUZKhRxPF3XUpBCjp4YzTKgLccjZhTSDM9YuVaPwxo (closed source). ARCHITECTURE: discrete price BINS (not tick-based CLMM). Price: (1 + binStep/10000)^binId. Bins grouped into BinArray accounts (70-100 bins); index = floor(binId/100). ACTIVE BIN: ONE active bin at market price; holds both tokens; earns ALL fees. Bins below = X only; above = Y only. POSITION: span of bins, liquidity shares per BinArray. Fees: baseFactor + variableFactor (volatility-scaled); fee bps = baseFactor x binStep. STRATEGIES: Spot (uniform); Curve (center); Bid-Ask (spread ends). TYPES: Permissionless; Permission (whitelist). ACCOUNTS: LbPair (pool), BinArray (liquidity), Position. DLMM vs CLMM: discrete prices, volatility fee.

M2A (arxiv 2602.07624): Multimodal Memory Agent with Dual-Layer Hybrid Memory. Feb 7, 2026. ARCHITECTURE: ChatAgent (ReAct: Query->Generate->Update) + MemoryManager (read-write only). ActMem + M2A research (2026-03-11). ACTMEM (arxiv 2603.00026): retrieval-reasoning gap -- NaiveRAG 84.86% retrieval but 61.54% QA. Architecture: unified causal+semantic KG; 4 modules: fact extraction -> clustering (cosine tau=0.2) -> KG (semantic edges cos>0.3 + causal PMI tau=0.8) -> two-pass counterfactual retrieval (vector search -> LLM generates what-would-follow -> second retrieval). ~10pp improvement over standard RAG on multi-hop QA. IMPLICATION FOR TORUS: counterfactual retrieval worth adding to search_knowledge; PMI-based causal edges complement Hebbian co-retrieval.

P2P GOSSIP+CRDT ARCHITECTURE (Option 5): KEY TECHS: libp2p (IPFS/Ethereum); GossipSub v1.1 (score-based peers, mesh propagation); IPFS Kademlia DHT (O(log N), CIDs SHA-256); OrbitDB (serverless P2P, Merkle-CRDTs, events/documents/keyvalue); GunDB (offline-first, commutative CRDT, SEA crypto). CRDT TYPES: state-based(full merge); operation-based(reliable delivery); Merkle-CRDTs(DAG, content-deduped). PEER DISCOVERY: bootstrap; Kademlia DHT; rendezvous; mDNS; GossipSub PRUNE. SYBIL RESISTANCE: GossipSub score; web-of-trust(BrightID); MeritRank decay; SEA PoW. SCALABILITY: O(k*logN) fanout 6-12; SpanningFL 30.7% comms reduction; GossipSub D=6(D_low=4,D_high=12). PROS: zero infra cost, censorship resistant. CONS: eventual consistency, hard quality enforcement, sybil risk, NAT traversal.

Session 220: Corrected Torus vs Clude comparison. Final: Torus 86.5 vs Clude 56.05 (+30.45). Previous undersold Torus—missed: memory linking, observation compaction, integrity_check.py, error_pattern_analyzer.py, 5-layer search, auto-tune. Torus dominates enforcement (10 vs 5), token efficiency (9 vs 4), consistency (10 vs 5), learning (9 vs 6), reliability (9 vs 5). Key: audit own capabilities thoroughly before comparing.

Correction #2: Torus has 6 distributed bond types—(1) Causal, (2) Semantic, (3) Temporal, (4) Contradictory, (5) Predictive (Markov), (6) Gate correlation. Distributed across: causal chain, tag co-retrieval, error_pattern_analyzer, gate_correlator, tool_patterns, security_profiles. Clude has 4 unified bonds. Torus Memory score: 9/10.

Bond architecture: Torus distributed (6 bonds/6 subsystems) 78.4 vs Clude unified 63.8 (+14.6). Clude wins elegance (8.6 vs 4.4)—unified graph easier to reason, native cross-queries. Torus wins operational: reliability (9 vs 5, no SPOF), token efficiency (8 vs 5), learning (9 vs 6, Markov predictive). Distributed=microservices (resilient, specialized). Unified=single-DB (elegant, fragile). At 1.3K scale, distributed strictly better.

Session 220 Final: Torus 84.0 vs Clude 55.2 (+28.8). Torus: 150+ components (18 gates, 47 modules, 21 tools, 6 bond types, 7 memory subsystems, 7 self-evolution, 39 skills, 1405+ tests). Clude: ~15 components. Clude wins: on-chain immutability, cross-agent sharing, elegance. Torus wins: enforcement (10 vs 5), token efficiency (9 vs 4), self-evolution (10 vs 2), observability (10), security (10 vs 3), testing. Verdict: Clude=brilliant prototype. Torus=production system.

Hive Judge vs Torus Gates (Session 190): Different philosophies — Torus PREVENTION (pre-execution, sys.exit(2), deterministic, zero LLM), Hive CORRECTION (post-execution, LLM judge, goal alignment, weighted scoring). Hive: EventLoopNode (per-turn) + HybridJudge (goal-level, 90% threshold REPLAN). Verdicts: ACCEPT/RETRY/ESCALATE/REPLAN. Torus strengths: prevents before action, deterministic, no LLM cost, fail-closed, 16 gates, causal chain. Hive strengths: goal alignment, natural criteria, evolution cycle (Execute→Evaluate→Diagnose→Regenerate). Ideal: combine both. Three ideas from Hive: (1) PostToolUse quality check, (2) session-level goal tracker, (3) REPLAN mechanism.

Gate 2 false positive tuning (2026-02-09). Two-layer filter: DANGEROUS_PATTERNS fires first, _is_safe_exception() checks if match is known-safe. Safe exceptions: source (venv activate, shell profiles), exec (python2/3/node/ruby/java/perl/npm/npx/cargo/go), DELETE FROM with WHERE clause, git stash drop with stash@{N}, here-strings (extracts command word before <<<). NO EXCEPTIONS: eval, bash -c, sh -c, pipe-to-shell, rsync --delete. Design: exec+rm -rf blocked because exec exception lets it through but rm -rf pattern catches next. 23 new tests (15 safe + 8 blocked), 126/126 passing. File: gate_02_no_destroy.py (lines 76-115).

SESSION 211 COMPARISON: Memory Server vs Enforcer Daemon UDS Resilience. ENFORCER (watchdog): _bind_socket helper, while True accept loop, PROACTIVE watchdog checks os.path.exists every ~1s, rebinds if missing, _shutting_down flag prevents rebind on shutdown. MEMORY SERVER (no watchdog): inlined bind, same accept loop, NO watchdog—socket.timeout just continues, never detects external deletion, reactive rebind on OSError. KEY: Memory server LACKS proactive watchdog. External socket deletion undetected until ~100 requests or OSError (~1s max enforcer latency).

Session 239 CRITICAL: git filter-repo --invert-paths on integrations/telegram-bot/config.json accidentally removed multiple hook files: enforcer_shim.py, failure_recovery.py, stop_cleanup.py, tg_mirror.py. filter-repo rewrites all commits/branches and removes files introduced in commits that become empty after filtering. Recovery: use git reflog to find pre-filter-repo commit hash and restore: git checkout OLD_HASH -- hooks/enforcer_shim.py hooks/failure_recovery.py hooks/stop_cleanup.py hooks/tg_mirror.py. Session blocked because enforcer_shim.py (PreToolUse hook) missing, blocking all Bash/Write/Edit. LESSON: Always test filter-repo on throwaway clone first.

Statusline cross-session state leak: _load_session_state() loads most-recent state file without session_id awareness; git branch cached to shared /tmp/statusline-git-cache (not session-specific). Vulnerable fields: git branch, error counts, verification ratio, test stats, session age. When Session A and B run back-to-back, B gets A's state. Root cause: statusline never calls state_file_for(session_id). Fix: add session_id param to _load_session_state() and get_git_branch().

Eclipse Agave #10 ExternalAccountDataModified error: stake program cannot modify governance-owned marker PDA (program only writes to owned accounts). FIX: Remove consume_emergency_marker() calls from stake_instruction dispatch and early_unlock(). Marker validation sufficient; marker becomes useless after unlock/deactivation without re-use risk: permanently locked changes deactivation_epoch from MAX (deactivate() won't trigger PERMANENTLY_LOCKED again); passive changes to Initialized (early_unlock() returns NotPassiveStake). Marker stays on-chain harmless.

[chainovi/eclipse-agave #10] Integration test fixes: (1) KeypairPubkeyMismatch: create_account_and_delegate_stake only needs mint_keypair and stake_keypair as signers, NOT vote_keypair (just referenced). (2) ExternalAccountDataModified: removed all consume_emergency_marker() calls—stake program can't write governance-owned accounts. State changes prevent re-use (deactivated accounts can't deactivate again). (3) Option::unwrap() panic in loaded_programs.rs:1062: must use `let (bank, _bank_forks)` NOT `let (bank, _)`. Underscore drops BankForks immediately, which drops fork_graph weak ref, causing panic when processing instructions. (4) Markers NOT zeroed by stake program, remain on-chain. State change is protection.

Idea: Lean on git log as ground truth to combat LIVE_STATE.json drift. Current problem: what_was_done hallucinations. Proposed: Hybrid approach — keep narrative but inject git log (git log -5 --oneline, git diff --stat HEAD~5, git status --short) in boot hook for cross-reference. Field mapping: what_was_done → git log (truth), feature → git branch (truth), uncommitted work → git status + diff (truth), known_issues/next_steps → keep as-is. Trade-off: lose narrative, gain drift-proof truth. Context: inspired by AKIRA killing LIVE_STATE vs our approach (killed HANDOFF.md).

[trading-framework] engine/executor.py created: Executor class (connector, gate_runner, portfolio, journal, notifier). execute(signal) converts signal to order, builds MarketData snapshot (prices, is_market_open), runs gate_runner, notifies if blocked, else submits order, journals trade, returns Order. Auto-sizing: strength * 0.05 * portfolio.total_value / price. engine/core.py created: Engine class (config, strategies, gate_runner, portfolio, journal). Creates Executor internally. run() creates CronScheduler from config (symbols, schedule_interval_seconds, candle_interval), wraps in KeyboardInterrupt. stop() calls scheduler.stop(). _on_candle catches exceptions, skips None/HOLD. Both verified importing OK.

Trading framework timeframe decision: Support all timeframes via scheduler abstraction. Strategy has on_candle()/on_tick() interface, doesn't know timeframe. Scheduler decides WHEN to call. Three scheduler types: Phase 1 (CronScheduler + REST daily/swing), Phase 2 (IntervalScheduler + REST intraday), Phase 3 (StreamScheduler + WebSocket scalping). "All timeframes" means engine supports all but different strategies per timeframe (trends ≠ noise ≠ microstructure). Share same interface. Drawbacks minimal if built in phases. Data: REST polling for daily/intraday, WebSocket for streaming/scalping. Total: ~8 hours across phases.

[chainovi/eclipse-agave #9] Emergency Unlock Marker System Complete Technical Spec. GOVERNANCE PROGRAM ID: Governance1111111111111111111111111111111111. PDA DERIVATION: seeds [b"emergency_unlock", stake_pubkey.as_ref()], program Governance, Pubkey::find_program_address(). [chainovi/eclipse-agave] Token migration from Torus Economy (C++ Bitcoin-fork): Two approaches: 1) AUTOMATIC: snapshot UTXO at cutoff, registration period for new pubkeys, genesis seeding. 2) MANUAL: burn-and-claim with off-chain oracle (secp256k1→ed25519). Key challenges: cross-curve verify, address mapping, exchange rate. Simplest: snapshot+registration+genesis seeding (no smart contract, just conversion script). Expect 60-80% migration rate.

Claude Code MCP has THREE config layers: (1) Global ~/.claude/mcp.json, (2) Project .mcp.json in repo root, (3) Local ~/.claude.json under projects."<path>".mcpServers (private, managed via `claude mcp add/remove -s local`). Local scope is default for `claude mcp add` without -s flag. Commands: `claude mcp list` (all servers), `claude mcp get <name>` (scope), `claude mcp remove "<name>" -s local`, `claude mcp add -s local <name> -- <cmd> <args>`.

DECISION: Built MCP approach. Hook layer could be added later as nudge-only.

Circuit breaker module (/home/crab/.claude/hooks/shared/circuit_breaker.py): States—CLOSED (normal), OPEN (reject), HALF_OPEN (probe). Defaults: threshold=5, timeout=60s, success_threshold=2. Persist: /dev/shm/claude-hooks/circuit_breaker.json (ramdisk), fallback ~/.claude/hooks/.circuit_breaker.json. Thread-safe: threading.Lock + atomic rename. API: record_success(), record_failure(), is_open(), get_state(), get_all_states(), reset(). Fail-open. Transitions: CLOSED→OPEN (failures>=threshold), OPEN→HALF_OPEN (timeout), HALF_OPEN→CLOSED (success>=2), HALF_OPEN→OPEN (fail). Test: 16/16 pass. Framework: 1124 pass (76 pre-existing fail, no regressions).

Enhanced circuit_breaker.py with gate system integration. Added _GATE_STATE_PATH (~/.claude/hooks/.circuit_breaker_state.json), gate-specific constants (_GATE_CRASH_THRESHOLD=3, _GATE_CRASH_WINDOW=300s, _GATE_COOLDOWN=60s, _GATE_SUCCESS_NEEDED=1), per-gate record schema (state, crash_timestamps, opened_at, total_crashes, total_skips), private helpers (_load_gate_state, _save_gate_state, _default_gate_record, _get_or_create_gate, _prune_crash_window, _gate_maybe_recover). Public API: should_skip_gate(gate_name)->bool, record_gate_result(gate_name, success), get_gate_circuit_state(gate_name)->str, reset_gate_circuit(gate_name), get_all_gate_states()->Dict. Design: fail-open, thread-safe, atomic writes. Tests: 16/16 smoke + 19/19 gate integration = 1182/1212 pass (30 pre-existing failures).

Trading framework gate architecture: Python functions embedded in engine, not bash or Claude hooks. Why: Trading gates need real computation (numpy, pandas correlation, Sharpe ratio, drawdown). Architecture: TradingGate base class with check(order, portfolio, market) → GateResult. Tier 1 (hard block): max position size, daily loss limit, capital, market closed. Tier 2 (override-able): no thesis, not backtested, cooldown, correlation high. Tier 3 (advisory): large order, off-hours, underperforming. From AKIRA: binary block/allow, fail-closed, data-driven, compliance. From torus: numbered testable gates, memory (learn from trades), causal chain (thesis→entry→outcome), tiered severity.

Benchmark: gate_performance.py (15 active gates, 1000 iterations). Results (2026-02-20): avg p95=0.33ms. gate_11_rate_limit needs optimization (p95=3.64ms, calls save_state() per invocation). Fastest: gate_09 (0.001ms), gate_06 (0.003ms), gate_05 (0.006ms). Pipeline estimate (11 gates): p50=0.81ms, p95=4.24ms. Design: importlib.import_module(), one realistic allow-path per gate, shallow dict.copy() state, reports p50/p95/p99/min/max/mean, flags p95>1ms, JSON output.

RESEARCH: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns. AgentOrchestra (arxiv:2506.12508): hierarchical planner + specialized sub-agents (web, analysis, file ops), TEA Protocol, 89.04% GAIA. AdaptOrch (arxiv:2602.16873 Feb2026): dynamically select 4 topologies (parallel, sequential, hierarchical, hybrid) per task, 12-23% improvement over static. Key: no single topology best. Multi-Agent Collaboration (arxiv:2505.19591): puppeteer paradigm with RL-trained orchestrator. OrchVis (arxiv:2510.24937): hierarchical alignment + oversight. For Torus: AdaptOrch critical — select torus-loop.sh vs torus-wave.py dynamically, not predetermined.

AgentEvolver (arXiv:2511.10395): Three mechanisms: (1) Self-Questioning—curiosity-driven tasks reduce handcrafted data, (2) Self-Navigating—improves exploration via experience reuse+hybrid policy, (3) Self-Attributing—contribution-weighted rewards not uniform. Torus applicability: Self-Attributing=causal chain tracking (different events have different weight), Self-Questioning=gate-generated tasks, Experience reuse=memory-first. Pattern: enhance causal chain with contribution_weight field. Backpropagate credit to record_attempt() steps—surfaces which steps matter.

MetaAgent (arXiv:2508.00271): Learning-by-doing—expertise via practice. Gaps→natural language help→best tool. Self-reflects+verifies post-task, distills to actionable text, persistent knowledge base. No parameter updates. Torus applicability: gates already verify claims; missing auto-distillation from gate fires. Pattern: after successful fix verified by Bash, auto-call remember_this() with "Fix for [error]: [strategy]. Verified by: [test hash]." Mirrors post-task reflection without manual effort.

ARXIV: SAGE - Self-evolving Agents (arXiv:2409.00872). Combines self-reflection + memory augmentation. Agents reflect on past, store distilled knowledge, retrieve to improve future performance. 2.26X improvement closed-source, 57.7%–100% open-source, large gains on smaller models. Torus applicability: validates memory-first+causal chain. Smaller models gain proportionally more from gate logic. Implementable: Add post-task reflection hook. After session_end, compare (a) what memory said fix would be vs (b) what actually worked (fix_outcomes). If discrepancy>threshold, generate correction entry with type:correction tag. Closes loop: Memory→Act→Reflect→Update Memory.

ARXIV: Agent-R (arXiv:2501.11425) & Multi-Agent Reflexion (MAR, arXiv:2512.20845). Agent-R: iterative self-training, on-the-fly reflection, prevents error loops, +5.59% baseline. MAR: extends to multi-agent with diverse personas + judge model synthesizing critiques; acting/diagnosing/critiquing/aggregating. HotPotQA +3, HumanEval +6.2. Torus applicability: Agent-R prevents Gate 6 deadlock. MAR's separation maps to agent/gate/mentor. Implementable: When gate 6 fires repeatedly (deadlock), spawn reflection sub-agent diagnosing WHY. Store diagnosis to LanceDB with type:error. Reset gate6_warn_count only if diagnosis identifies genuine fix, not bypass.

ARXIV: JitRL (arXiv:2601.18510) & Lifelong Learning Roadmap (arXiv:2501.07278). JitRL: training-free, frozen LLMs adapt at test-time via logit optimization, gradient-free, continual learning without updates. Lifelong Roadmap: 3 pillars—Perception (multimodal), Memory (evolving store), Action (grounded). Core challenge: catastrophic forgetting. Torus: gates run inference-time; JitRL means gates adjust dynamically based on session signal without retraining (threshold warm: tight early, relax as track record improves). Memory Module exists (LanceDB); missing: catastrophic forgetting prevention. Implementable: preserve_score field in LanceDB. High scores survive compaction. Score increases on successful retrieval/use. Compactor deletes preserve_score<threshold AND age>max_age only.

HyMem Research (arxiv:2602.13933, Feb 2026): Hybrid Memory Architecture with Dynamic Retrieval Scheduling. Dual-granular storage: L1 (event-level summaries: context/time/location/participants) + L2 (raw dialogue). Explicit L1→L2 links. Two-tier: (1) Lightweight—cosine L1, low latency. (2) Deep—conditional, ~22% activation. Coarse semantic recall → LLM self-retrieval → L2 backtrack. Reflection: completeness check, decompose to sub-problems. Benchmarks (gpt-4o-mini): LOCOMO 89.55% vs Full 77.51% vs Zep 75.14%; multi-hop 88.16% vs 46.89%; token cost 1.5k vs 21.4k (92.6% reduction). LongMemEval-s: 75.00% vs LightMem 68.64%. Weakness: no write-time consolidation, completeness choke point, no cross-session evolution.

HyMem (arxiv:2602.13933, Feb 2026): Dual-granularity (summary/raw, NOT episodic/semantic). L1: summaries (context, time, participants); L2: raw text (full fidelity). Two-tier retrieval: T1 (lightweight, cosine L1, completeness eval); T2 (deep, conditional, ~22% activation, coarse recall → LLM self-retrieval → L2 backtrack, decomposes unresolved to sub-queries). Benchmarks (gpt-4o-mini): LOCOMO 89.55% (multi-hop 88.16% vs 46.89% full), single-hop 85.15%, temporal 92.98%, token 1.5k vs 21.4k (92.6% reduction). LongMemEval-s 75.00%. Strengths: SOTA, 92.6% reduction, elegant backtracking. Weaknesses: no write-time consolidation, completeness bottleneck, no cross-session evolution, misses semantic abstraction.

Trading gate system: 12 gates, 3 tiers. TIER 1 (hard block, fail-closed): Gate 1—Position size ≤5% portfolio, Gate 2—Daily loss ≤-3%, Gate 3—Insufficient balance, Gate 4—Market closed (crypto always open). TIER 2 (block+override, fail-open): Gate 5—Thesis required, Gate 6—Cooldown (3 losses→30min), Gate 7—Max 5 open positions, Gate 8—Correlation >0.7 guard, Gate 9—Backtest proof. TIER 3 (advisory, logs only): Gate 10—Unusual size >2x avg, Gate 11—Off-hours, Gate 12—Win rate <40% last 20. Runner: sorted by tier, T1/T2 block, T3 log. Fail-closed block on crash, fail-open log+continue. All configurable. Phase 1: gates 1,2,3,4,6,7 (capital+discipline); later: 5,8,9; then 10,11,12.

FTS5 Persistence Analysis (Session 22). BENEFITS: faster cold start (26ms now→~650ms at 5000 vs ~2ms persistent), future-proofing, reduced ChromaDB load. RISKS: staleness/desync if ChromaDB changes externally (high), crash corruption (medium), sync validation+recovery complexity (medium). ZERO token/context impact—FTS5 server-side only. Trade-off: lose "always in sync" guarantee from in-memory rebuild. Dual-write pattern already in place (lines 773-774). DECISION: Hold at current threshold; revisit at ~800 memories. Currently 195 entries, rebuild is 26ms (imperceptible). Persistence worth complexity at ~800-1000 entries.
